common_name: Sylvester Date Palm (Silver Date Palm, Wild Date Palm)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring when soil is warm (70°F+); Sylvester Date Palm establishes best in warm soil. Purchase the largest container size affordable — palms cannot branch to compensate for a frozen or damaged growing tip, so a wider, established trunk is more cold-resilient in zone 8b. Space 15–20 feet from structures. Plant with the root ball slightly above grade in clay soils to prevent crown rot.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — clay, sand, alkaline, or loam — Sylvester Date Palm is broadly adaptable and extremely drought-tolerant once established; avoid waterlogged soils. Phoenix sylvestris (Wild Date Palm or Silver Date Palm) is native to Pakistan, India, and Sri Lanka, where it is heavily tapped for the sweet sap used to produce date palm jaggery, a prized molasses-like sweetener throughout South Asia. In Gulf Coast zone 9 landscapes it is one of the most dramatic and widely-planted feather palms, prized for its striking silvery blue-green fronds — significantly more silver-blue than the commercial Date Palm (P. dactylifera) — and its distinctive diamond-patterned trunk that becomes increasingly textured and photogenic with age. Unlike P. dactylifera, Sylvester Date Palm tolerates more cold (zone 8b, to about 20–22°F with brief exposure), adapts to the high humidity and rainfall of the Gulf South, and resists the root and crown rots that plague commercial date palms in wet climates. The palm produces clusters of small, yellow to orange-red date-like fruits that ripen in fall and are edible fresh or used for making jaggery syrup. An outstanding statement palm for large residential properties and commercial streetscapes in zone 9, frequently planted in staggered triple groups for maximum visual impact.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Phoenix sylvestris (Sylvester Date Palm)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Palms for Texas: Phoenix species
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Phoenix sylvestris cultivation notes
Morton, J.F. (1987) Fruits of Warm Climates — Wild Date Palm

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after last frost; Kei Apple is dioecious — separate male and female plants are required for fruit production. Plant at least one male per 5–8 females for good pollination. Female plants are identified by their flowers (pistillate, without stamens). Grafted female selections on male rootstocks are available from specialty nurseries and remove the uncertainty of seedling-grown plants.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — Kei Apple tolerates poor, sandy, alkaline, and mildly saline soils and is extremely drought-tolerant once established. Dovyalis caffra is a large thorny shrub or small tree native to the coastal thornveld and thicket of South Africa's Eastern Cape province, named for the Kei River region where it grows wild. The formidable 2-inch spines make it one of the most impenetrable barrier plants available for zone 9 — a mature hedge is impassable to deer, livestock, and intruders. The small, round fruits (3/4 to 1 inch diameter) ripen in summer (July–September in zone 9) to a bright golden-yellow or amber color with paper-thin skin and juicy, very acidic pulp that is rarely eaten fresh but makes extraordinary jams, jellies, chutney, and preserves with a distinctive bright, tart flavor combining notes of apricot, plum, and citrus. The glossy, willow-like evergreen foliage is ornamentally attractive year-round. Kei Apple can be grown as a specimen tree (2–4 trunks) or planted 4–6 feet apart and clipped into a dense, absolutely impenetrable hedge. Drought tolerance, extreme heat resistance, and salt spray tolerance make it valuable for coastal Gulf South gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dovyalis caffra (Kei Apple)
California Rare Fruit Growers — Kei Apple profile
Morton, J.F. (1987) Fruits of Warm Climates — Kei Apple
South African National Biodiversity Institute — Dovyalis caffra

common_name: Standing Winecup (Finger Poppy Mallow, Tall Winecup)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (October–November in zone 9) for spring blooming; established plants develop a deep, fleshy taproot that resents transplanting — direct seeding in permanent position is strongly preferred. In zone 9, fall-sown seed germinates in cool winter weather and plants bloom April–June. Excellent drainage is essential; plants rot in heavy clay or poorly drained soils. Do not mulch deeply over the crown.

directions: Direct sow in very well-drained, lean, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; rich soils produce weak, floppy stems with reduced flowering. Callirhoe digitata is an upright native prairie perennial from the tallgrass and mixed prairies of Oklahoma, Kansas, and Texas, distinguished from the sprawling Winecup (C. involucrata) by its erect, branching habit on wiry stems reaching 2–3 feet, and by its deeply digitately-lobed leaves that resemble a spread hand (digitata = finger-like). The vivid magenta-crimson flowers are identical in brilliance to those of its sprawling relative and bloom April–July. The plant develops a deep, fleshy carrot-shaped taproot that stores water and nutrients — allowing well-established plants to survive extreme summer drought without irrigation and to regrow vigorously from the root crown when the above-ground portion dies back in heat. Once the taproot is established (2nd year), the plants are essentially indestructible in appropriate well-drained soils. Native American peoples reportedly used the taproots as a cooked vegetable. Standing Winecup self-seeds modestly in suitable conditions, slowly naturalizing in dry, gravelly garden spots. An outstanding choice for dry, sunny slopes, rock gardens, and xeriscape plantings in zone 9 where summer drought eliminates most other flowering plants.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Callirhoe digitata (Standing Winecup)
Native Plant Society of Texas — Callirhoe digitata
USDA Plants Database — Callirhoe digitata
Kansas Native Plant Society — Tallgrass prairie wildflowers

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring when soil is warm; Cardboard Palm grows slowly — a single new set of leaves per year is normal. Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade. In zone 9a, protect from extended hard freezes; the foliage may brown below 25°F but established plants typically resprout from the crown. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested — keep away from children and pets.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, sandy or gritty soil; Cardboard Palm is extremely drought-tolerant once established and will rot in wet, poorly-drained conditions. Zamia furfuracea is a cycad (an ancient gymnosperm, not a true palm) native to the coastal sand dunes and scrub of Veracruz, Mexico. It is one of the most widely planted landscape cycads in Gulf Coast and Florida gardens, prized for its symmetrical rosette of tough, leathery, dark olive-green fronds with broad, felt-covered leaflets that have the exact texture and appearance of corrugated cardboard — the characteristic that gives the plant its common name. The extremely slow growth rate (one complete flush of new leaves per year), prehistoric architectural form, and rugged adaptability to dry, poor, salt-exposed coastal soils make it an ideal low-maintenance specimen for zone 9 Gulf Coast landscapes. Cardboard Palm is an excellent partial-shade plant under mature trees where other ornamentals fail — its deep-green leaves brighten shaded spots and it tolerates the root competition and dry shade of live oak canopies. CAUTION: All parts of the plant are toxic to humans, dogs, and cats — the seeds and starchy trunk contain macrozamin and other cycasin compounds that cause liver failure.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Zamia furfuracea (Cardboard Palm)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Zamia furfuracea
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Cycads for Texas Landscapes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center — Zamia species toxicity

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring; Guadalupe Palm is slow-growing and may take 5–10 years to develop a visible trunk from a container plant. It is more cold-hardy than most feather palms and more drought-tolerant than other Brahea species. Plant in a hot, sunny, extremely well-drained spot — it fails in wet clay soils.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained soil; Guadalupe Palm requires excellent drainage and is highly drought-tolerant once established — it will not tolerate poorly-drained or consistently moist soils. Brahea edulis is a single-trunk fan palm endemic to Guadalupe Island, a remote volcanic island 150 miles off the Baja California coast of Mexico, where it survived in harsh, wind-scoured, fog-desert conditions for millennia. In cultivation it has proven to be among the most drought-tolerant and cold-hardy fan palms available for zone 9 Gulf Coast and California gardens — surviving brief dips to 20°F and thriving in the extreme summer heat and drought of zone 9. The crown of large, fan-shaped, bright mid-green fronds has a full, lush appearance that contrasts beautifully with the more compact, gray-green look of Mexican Blue Palm (Brahea armata). Guadalupe Palm produces edible dates — round, black, cherry-sized fruits with sweet, date-like flesh that was an important food for the indigenous people of Guadalupe Island and is still eaten fresh or dried. The species is rare in its native habitat due to feral goat browsing and is considered vulnerable in the wild. In zone 9 landscapes, it is a prized specimen palm for dry gardens and xeriscape plantings.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Brahea edulis (Guadalupe Palm)
California Rare Fruit Growers — Guadalupe Palm profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Brahea species comparative notes
IUCN Red List — Brahea edulis (Guadalupe Island endemic palms)
